lessons.title: <stdfloat>: Fixed-Width Floating-Point Types
lessons.motivation: The types `float`, `double`, and `long double` have existed in C++ since its C heritage, but they carry a fundamental flaw: the standard never guaranteed their exact sizes in bytes. On most x86 platforms `float` is 32-bit IEEE 754 and `double` is 64-bit, but this is a de-facto norm rather than a language requirement. On certain embedded and DSP platforms the picture is different. For code that needs exact IEEE 754 semantics — network protocols, file formats, machine-learning libraries — this ambiguity causes portability problems. C++23 addresses this by introducing the `<stdfloat>` header with five new fixed-width floating-point types.
lessons.theory:
Before C++23, programmers who needed guaranteed precision wrote `typedef float float32_t;` — but that is merely an alias, not a new type. Two aliases for the same type cannot serve as the basis for function overloading, and the compiler cannot distinguish `float32_t` from `float`. C++23 solves this by introducing truly distinct types in the language's type system.

The `<stdfloat>` header defines up to five types: `std::float16_t` (IEEE 754 binary16, half precision), `std::float32_t` (IEEE 754 binary32, single precision — matches `float` on most platforms), `std::float64_t` (IEEE 754 binary64, double precision), `std::float128_t` (IEEE 754 binary128, quad precision — requires hardware support), and `std::bfloat16_t` (Brain Float 16, a format developed by Google/Intel). The standard only provides a type if the platform supports it; on x86-64 with GCC/Clang, all four except `float128_t` are typically available.

`bfloat16_t` deserves special attention. Unlike `float16_t`, which has 5 exponent bits and 10 mantissa bits, `bfloat16_t` has the same 8 exponent bits as `float32_t` but only 7 mantissa bits. This means the same value range as `float32_t` at half the memory footprint. That is precisely why Google TPU, Intel AMX, TensorFlow, and PyTorch use bfloat16 for neural-network weights: training stability demands a wide dynamic range (to avoid overflow/underflow), not maximum mantissa precision.

In practice all new types support arithmetic operators and work with `std::numeric_limits<T>`. `std::format` and `std::print` format them correctly. Initializing from `float` literals requires an explicit cast (`static_cast`) — implicit narrowing in brace-initialization is forbidden. The key advantage over the old `typedef` approach is that these types are distinct in the type system, enabling overloaded functions that accept `float16_t` and `float32_t` as different arguments.

examples.title: Sizes and precision of the new types
examples.description: Checking the sizes of all available types from `<stdfloat>` and their precision via `std::numeric_limits`.
examples.expectedOutput:
sizeof float16_t:  2
sizeof float32_t:  4
sizeof float64_t:  8
sizeof bfloat16_t: 2
float32_t max digits10: 9
float64_t max digits10: 17
float16_t max digits10: 5

examples.title: bfloat16 for storing neural-network weights
examples.description: A practical ML scenario: storing weights in `bfloat16_t` instead of `float32_t` halves memory usage. Arithmetic is performed via explicit cast to `float`.
examples.expectedOutput:
bfloat16 storage: 8 bytes for 4 weights
float32 storage:  16 bytes for 4 weights
sum of bf16 weights: 1.375
float16_t == float32_t value: true

lessons.title: `std::byteswap`, `std::to_underlying`, `std::unreachable`: Low-Level Utilities
lessons.motivation: Before C++23, reversing the bytes of an integer meant writing `__builtin_bswap32(x)` on GCC/Clang or `_byteswap_ulong(x)` on MSVC. Marking a code path as unreachable required `__builtin_unreachable()` or `__assume(false)`. Extracting the underlying value from an `enum class` demanded the verbose `static_cast<std::underlying_type_t<Color>>(value)`. Each of these patterns solved a real problem, but with non-standard syntax: the code was non-portable and littered the codebase with platform-specific details. C++23 standardizes all three operations as clean, `constexpr`-ready functions.
lessons.theory:
Historically C++ inherited from C a large number of useful low-level operations that remained outside the standard — each compiler supplied its own extensions. C++20 began the cleanup: `<bit>` gained `std::popcount`, `std::rotl`, `std::rotr`. C++23 adds three more utilities, closing the most common pain points.

`std::byteswap` (header `<bit>`, P1272) reverses the byte order of any integral type. It accepts any `integral` type — `uint8_t`, `uint16_t`, `uint32_t`, `uint64_t`, `int`, and so on — and returns the same type with its bytes swapped. The function is `constexpr`, so the computation can happen at compile time. Typical use cases: converting between network byte order (big-endian protocol) and host byte order (little-endian), reading binary file formats, and certain hashing algorithms. Before C++23 the alternatives were `__builtin_bswap32` / `__builtin_bswap64` (GCC/Clang) or `_byteswap_ulong` (MSVC), or manual bitwise arithmetic.

`std::unreachable` (header `<utility>`, P0627) marks a point in the code as unreachable. If execution actually reaches that point, the behavior is undefined. The compiler uses this promise for optimization: it can eliminate checks in `switch` defaults, shrink the control-flow graph, and remove dead branches. The function is especially useful after an exhaustive `switch` over an `enum` — it eliminates the "not all paths return a value" warning and gives the optimizer complete information. **Important:** unlike `assert()`, `std::unreachable` performs no runtime check in either debug or release builds. If the unreachability assumption is wrong, the program has silent undefined behavior. Use it only where you are absolutely certain about the invariant.

`std::to_underlying` (header `<utility>`, P1682) returns an enumeration value cast to its underlying type. It works with both `enum class` and plain `enum`, and returns `std::underlying_type_t<E>` — always the exact type declared as the enum's base. This function was covered in detail in lesson m1-l5; it is worth noting here that it rounds out the set alongside `byteswap` and `unreachable` as the third low-level C++23 utility, replacing the verbose `static_cast<std::underlying_type_t<E>>(e)` with a concise call.

examples.title: std::byteswap: byte-order conversion
examples.description: Converting between host byte order and network byte order with `std::byteswap`. The function works in a `constexpr` context — a port number can be converted at compile time. `to_underlying` lets you print a numeric HTTP status code without a verbose `static_cast`.
examples.expectedOutput:
host:    0x12345678
network: 0x78563412
round-trip: 0x12345678
port 8080 in network order: 0x901f
HTTP code: 404

examples.title: std::unreachable: exhaustive switch and the optimizer
examples.description: `std::unreachable()` after the last `case` tells the optimizer that the default branch can never execute. This eliminates the compiler warning about "not all paths returning a value" and helps the code generator produce tighter output.
examples.expectedOutput:
direction: North
direction: South
direction: East
direction: West
sign(42): 1
sign(-7): -1
sign(0): 0
